kids.question: How did God speak to people long ago?
kids.option_a: Through the prophets
kids.explanation: Hebrews 1:1 — "God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets."

kids.question: How does God speak to us now?
kids.option_a: Through his Son, Jesus
kids.explanation: Hebrews 1:2 — "Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son."

kids.question: Is Jesus greater than the angels?
kids.option_a: Yes, he is much greater than the angels
kids.explanation: Hebrews 1:4 — "Being made so much better than the angels, as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they."

teens.question: What did God appoint his Son heir of?
teens.option_a: All things — and by whom also he made the worlds
teens.option_c: Only the church
teens.explanation: Hebrews 1:2 — "Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds."

teens.question: What is the Son described as being in relation to God's glory?
teens.option_a: The brightness of his glory and the express image of his person
teens.option_b: A faint reflection of God's power
teens.option_c: A distant shadow of God's nature
teens.explanation: Hebrews 1:3 — "Who being the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person."

teens.question: What does the Son uphold?
teens.option_a: All things by the word of his power
teens.option_c: Only the nation of Israel
teens.explanation: Hebrews 1:3 — "Upholding all things by the word of his power."

teens.question: What did God say to the Son that he never said to any angel?
teens.option_a: Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee
teens.option_c: Thou art a faithful servant
teens.explanation: Hebrews 1:5 — "For unto which of the angels said he at any time, Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee?"

teens.question: What are angels described as?
teens.option_a: Ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation
teens.option_c: Equal partners with God
teens.option_d: Beings who do not serve humans
teens.explanation: Hebrews 1:14 — "Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation?"

adults.question: How does the author describe God's progressive revelation?
adults.option_a: God spoke at sundry times and in divers manners in time past by the prophets, but in these last days hath spoken by his Son
adults.option_b: God spoke once through Moses and never again
adults.option_c: God speaks only through written Scripture
adults.option_d: God speaks identically in every era
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:1-2 — "God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets, Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son."

adults.question: What did the Son do after purging our sins?
adults.option_a: He sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high
adults.option_b: He returned to walk among men
adults.option_c: He continued offering sacrifices
adults.option_d: He went to prepare additional tabernacles
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:3 — "When he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high."

adults.question: What does God say to the Son in verse 8?
adults.option_a: Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom
adults.option_b: Thou art the greatest of the angels
adults.option_c: Thy kingdom shall last a thousand years
adults.option_d: Thou art the firstborn of creation
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:8 — "But unto the Son he saith, Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom."

adults.question: What does God say the Son has been anointed with above his fellows?
adults.option_a: The oil of gladness — because he loved righteousness and hated iniquity
adults.option_b: The oil of anointing from the temple
adults.option_c: The water of baptism
adults.option_d: The blood of the covenant
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:9 — "Thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity; therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows."

adults.question: What does God say about the Son and creation in verses 10-12?
adults.option_a: Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth — they shall perish but thou remainest; they shall be changed but thou art the same
adults.option_b: The Son was created along with the heavens
adults.option_c: Creation is eternal and unchanging
adults.option_d: The earth will never perish or change
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:10-12 — "Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth; and the heavens are the works of thine hands: They shall perish; but thou remainest... thou art the same, and thy years shall not fail."

adults.question: What question does the author ask about the Son and angels in verse 13?
adults.option_a: To which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand until I make thine enemies thy footstool?
adults.option_b: Which angel was given dominion over creation?
adults.option_c: Which angel was sent to redeem Israel?
adults.option_d: Which angel is the mediator of the new covenant?
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:13 — "But to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool?"

adults.question: What Psalm does the author quote to address the Son as God?
adults.option_a: Psalm 45:6-7 — "Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever" — directly applying the title "God" to the Son
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.option_c: Psalm 119 — Blessed are the undefiled
adults.option_d: Psalm 51 — Create in me a clean heart
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes Psalm 45:6-7, applying the title "O God" directly to the Son, affirming his deity.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the Son's name being "more excellent" than the angels?
adults.option_a: The Son's superiority is by inheritance — he possesses divine nature and authority that no created being can share, establishing his deity and supremacy
adults.option_b: The Son earned a better name through good behaviour
adults.option_c: The name difference is merely ceremonial
adults.option_d: Angels and the Son share equal nature but different titles
adults.explanation: Hebrews 1:4 — "Being made so much better than the angels, as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they." The inherited name signifies essential divine nature, not earned status.

kids.question: Why should we pay attention to what we have heard about Jesus?
kids.option_a: So we don't drift away from the truth
kids.option_c: So we can win a contest
kids.option_d: So we can be smarter than everyone
kids.explanation: Hebrews 2:1 — "Therefore we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time we should let them slip."

kids.question: What did Jesus taste for every person?
kids.option_a: Death — he died for everyone
kids.explanation: Hebrews 2:9 — "That he by the grace of God should taste death for every man."

kids.question: Is Jesus ashamed to call believers his brothers and sisters?
kids.option_a: No, he is not ashamed to call them brethren
kids.option_c: He doesn't know who they are
kids.option_d: He only calls the apostles his brethren
kids.explanation: Hebrews 2:11 — "For which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren."

teens.question: What does the author call the salvation through Christ?
teens.option_a: So great salvation — which began to be spoken by the Lord and was confirmed by them that heard him
teens.option_c: An optional extra for believers
teens.explanation: Hebrews 2:3 — "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation; which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him."

teens.question: Why was Jesus made a little lower than the angels?
teens.option_a: For the suffering of death — that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man
teens.option_c: Because he was not truly God
teens.option_d: Because he needed to learn from angels
teens.explanation: Hebrews 2:9 — "But we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour; that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man."

teens.question: What did Jesus destroy through his death?
teens.option_a: Him that had the power of death, that is, the devil
teens.option_c: The temple in Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Hebrews 2:14 — "That through death he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil."

teens.question: Who did Jesus come to help — angels or humans?
teens.option_a: The seed of Abraham — he took not on him the nature of angels
teens.option_c: Both equally
teens.explanation: Hebrews 2:16 — "For verily he took not on him the nature of angels; but he took on him the seed of Abraham."

teens.question: Why can Jesus help those who are tempted?
teens.option_a: Because he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted
teens.option_b: Because he has never experienced temptation
teens.option_c: Because he uses angelic power to help
teens.option_d: Because he only helps the strong
teens.explanation: Hebrews 2:18 — "For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted."

adults.question: What rhetorical question does the author pose about neglecting salvation?
adults.option_a: How shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation?
adults.option_b: How shall we be rewarded for our good works?
adults.option_c: How long must we wait for the second coming?
adults.option_d: How can we improve the law of Moses?
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:3 — "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation; which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him."

adults.question: What title does the author give Jesus regarding salvation?
adults.option_a: The captain of their salvation, made perfect through sufferings
adults.option_b: The general of heaven's armies
adults.option_c: The king of earthly kingdoms
adults.option_d: The architect of the universe
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:10 — "For it became him, for whom are all things, and by whom are all things, in bringing many sons unto glory, to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings."

adults.question: What does the author say about the sanctifier and those sanctified?
adults.option_a: They are all of one, for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren
adults.option_b: They are of entirely different natures
adults.option_c: The sanctified can never approach the sanctifier
adults.option_d: They have no relationship to each other
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:11 — "For both he that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one: for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren."

adults.question: Why did Christ partake of flesh and blood?
adults.option_a: That through death he might destroy him that had the power of death — the devil — and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
adults.option_b: To enjoy the pleasures of human life
adults.option_c: To demonstrate human frailty
adults.option_d: To experience aging and natural death
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:14-15 — "Forasmuch then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same; that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil; And deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage."

adults.question: What role is Jesus said to fulfil as a result of being made like his brethren?
adults.option_a: A merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people
adults.option_b: A political ruler over the nations
adults.option_c: A military commander against Rome
adults.option_d: A philosophical teacher of wisdom
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:17 — "Wherefore in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people."

adults.question: What Psalm does the author quote about man being crowned with glory and honour?
adults.option_a: Psalm 8:4-6 — "What is man, that thou art mindful of him?" applied to Christ's incarnation and exaltation
adults.option_b: Psalm 22 — My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me
adults.option_c: Psalm 110 — The LORD said unto my Lord
adults.option_d: Psalm 2 — Thou art my Son
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:6-8 quotes Psalm 8:4-6: "What is man, that thou art mindful of him? or the son of man, that thou visitest him? Thou madest him a little lower than the angels; thou crownedst him with glory and honour."

adults.question: What does the author say about "all things" being put under the Son?
adults.option_a: We see not yet all things put under him, but we see Jesus crowned with glory and honour — the "not yet" shows eschatological tension
adults.option_b: All things are already fully under his control visibly
adults.option_c: Nothing has been put under him yet
adults.option_d: Only spiritual things are under his authority
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:8-9 — "Thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet... But now we see not yet all things put under him. But we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour."

adults.question: What does "made perfect through sufferings" mean regarding the captain of salvation?
adults.option_a: Christ's sufferings completed and qualified him as the perfect Saviour — not moral improvement but the completion of his redemptive work through his sacrifice
adults.option_b: Christ was morally imperfect and needed suffering to be purified
adults.option_c: Suffering is required for all believers to be saved
adults.option_d: Christ's divine nature was incomplete until the cross
adults.explanation: Hebrews 2:10 — "To make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings." The Greek "teleiōsai" means to bring to completion — Christ's saving work was completed through the cross.

kids.question: Who should we think about and focus on?
kids.option_a: Jesus — the Apostle and High Priest of our profession
kids.explanation: Hebrews 3:1 — "Wherefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus."

kids.question: Who was faithful in God's house as a servant?
kids.option_a: Moses
kids.explanation: Hebrews 3:5 — "And Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant."

kids.question: What should we NOT do with our hearts?
kids.option_a: Harden them — we should keep our hearts soft toward God
kids.explanation: Hebrews 3:8 — "Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation."

teens.question: How is Christ counted worthy of more glory than Moses?
teens.option_a: As the builder of a house hath more honour than the house — Moses was faithful in the house, but Christ built the house
teens.option_b: Because Moses sinned and Christ did not
teens.option_c: Because Christ lived longer than Moses
teens.explanation: Hebrews 3:3-4 — "For this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, inasmuch as he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house. For every house is builded by some man; but he that built all things is God."

teens.question: What role did Moses have in God's house versus Christ?
teens.option_a: Moses was faithful as a servant, but Christ as a Son over his own house
teens.option_b: Moses was the owner and Christ was the builder
teens.option_c: Moses was the high priest and Christ was the king
teens.explanation: Hebrews 3:5-6 — "And Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant... But Christ as a son over his own house; whose house are we."

teens.question: What happened when Israel hardened their hearts in the wilderness?
teens.option_a: God swore in his wrath that they would not enter into his rest
teens.option_c: They conquered the promised land easily
teens.explanation: Hebrews 3:11 — "So I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest."

teens.question: What should believers do for each other daily?
teens.option_a: Exhort one another daily, lest any be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin
teens.option_b: Compete with one another for the best rewards
teens.option_c: Ignore one another's struggles
teens.explanation: Hebrews 3:13 — "But exhort one another daily, while it is called To day; lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin."

teens.question: What kept the Israelites from entering the promised land?
teens.option_a: Unbelief — they could not enter in because of unbelief
teens.option_b: A physical wall around the land
teens.option_c: A river too deep to cross
teens.option_d: An army too strong to defeat
teens.explanation: Hebrews 3:19 — "So we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief."

adults.question: What two titles does the author give Christ in verse 1?
adults.option_a: The Apostle and High Priest of our profession — the only time Christ is called "Apostle" in Scripture
adults.option_b: Prophet and King
adults.option_c: Shepherd and Bishop
adults.option_d: Teacher and Healer
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:1 — "Wherefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus."

adults.question: What does the author argue about Christ being the builder?
adults.option_a: He that built all things is God — identifying Christ as the divine builder of God's house, thus affirming his deity
adults.option_b: Christ was merely a skilled craftsman
adults.option_c: Moses was the true builder of God's house
adults.option_d: The house has no builder
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:4 — "For every house is builded by some man; but he that built all things is God."

adults.question: What condition does the author attach to being Christ's house?
adults.option_a: If we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end
adults.option_b: If we perform all the ritual requirements of the law
adults.option_c: If we achieve moral perfection in this life
adults.option_d: If we earn enough merit through good works
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:6 — "But Christ as a son over his own house; whose house are we, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end."

adults.question: What Psalm does the author quote extensively about hardening hearts?
adults.option_a: Psalm 95:7-11 — "To day if ye will hear his voice, Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation"
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.option_c: Psalm 51 — Have mercy upon me
adults.option_d: Psalm 119 — Thy word is a lamp
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:7-11 quotes Psalm 95:7-11 as the words of the Holy Ghost.

adults.question: What does the author warn about an evil heart of unbelief?
adults.option_a: Take heed lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God
adults.option_b: Unbelief is a minor issue easily overlooked
adults.option_c: Only certain people can have unbelief
adults.option_d: Unbelief affects the mind but not the heart
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:12 — "Take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in departing from the living God."

adults.question: What does it mean to be "partakers of Christ"?
adults.option_a: We are made partakers of Christ if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end
adults.option_b: We automatically share in Christ regardless of perseverance
adults.option_c: Only the apostles are partakers of Christ
adults.option_d: Partaking of Christ is a future event only
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:14 — "For we are made partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end."

adults.question: With whom was God grieved forty years?
adults.option_a: With them that had sinned, whose carcases fell in the wilderness
adults.option_b: With Moses and Aaron only
adults.option_c: With the Canaanites
adults.option_d: With the Egyptians who pursued them
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:17 — "But with whom was he grieved forty years? was it not with them that had sinned, whose carcases fell in the wilderness?"

adults.question: What is the relationship between sin, unbelief, and disobedience in this chapter?
adults.option_a: Sin leads to hardened hearts, unbelief causes departure from God, and disobedience prevents entering God's rest — all three are interconnected
adults.option_b: Sin and unbelief are unrelated concepts
adults.option_c: Only outward disobedience matters, not the heart
adults.option_d: Unbelief is acceptable if behaviour is correct
adults.explanation: Hebrews 3:12-13,18-19 links an evil heart of unbelief, the deceitfulness of sin, and disobedience as interconnected causes that prevented Israel from entering rest.

kids.question: What is the word of God described as?
kids.option_a: Quick (living) and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword
kids.option_c: A quiet whisper no one can hear
kids.explanation: Hebrews 4:12 — "For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword."

kids.question: Who is our great high priest in heaven?
kids.option_a: Jesus, the Son of God
kids.explanation: Hebrews 4:14 — "Seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God."

kids.question: How can we come to God for help?
kids.option_a: Boldly — we can come boldly to the throne of grace
kids.option_c: Only through a priest on earth
kids.explanation: Hebrews 4:16 — "Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace."

teens.question: What promise remains for the people of God?
teens.option_a: A rest — there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God
teens.option_c: An earthly kingdom
teens.option_d: A treasure of gold and silver
teens.explanation: Hebrews 4:9 — "There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God."

teens.question: What does the word of God pierce and divide?
teens.option_a: It pierces even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart
teens.option_b: Only the written text of the law
teens.option_c: Only the physical world around us
teens.option_d: Only the outward actions of people
teens.explanation: Hebrews 4:12 — "Piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart."

teens.question: Can our high priest sympathize with our weaknesses?
teens.option_a: Yes — he was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin
teens.option_b: No, he has never experienced human weakness
teens.option_c: Only with some of our weaknesses
teens.option_d: He understands but chooses not to help
teens.explanation: Hebrews 4:15 — "For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin."

teens.question: What do we find when we come boldly to the throne of grace?
teens.option_a: Mercy and grace to help in time of need
teens.option_c: A list of our failures
teens.explanation: Hebrews 4:16 — "Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need."

teens.question: Why did the gospel not profit the Israelites who heard it?
teens.option_a: Because it was not mixed with faith in them that heard it
teens.option_b: Because Moses didn't explain it clearly
teens.option_c: Because the desert was too hot for worship
teens.option_d: Because they were too tired from travelling
teens.explanation: Hebrews 4:2 — "For unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them: but the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard it."

adults.question: What does the author mean by God's rest that remains?
adults.option_a: A spiritual Sabbath rest for the people of God — he that is entered into his rest hath ceased from his own works as God did from his
adults.option_b: Only the physical land of Canaan
adults.option_c: Only the weekly Sabbath observance
adults.option_d: Only the rest experienced after death
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:9-10 — "There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his."

adults.question: What four attributes does the author give to the word of God?
adults.option_a: Quick (living), powerful, sharper than any twoedged sword, and a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart
adults.option_b: Ancient, complex, difficult, and mysterious
adults.option_c: Simple, clear, brief, and historical
adults.option_d: Poetic, musical, artistic, and cultural
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:12 — "For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit... and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart."

adults.question: What does "neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight" mean?
adults.option_a: All things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do — complete divine omniscience leaving nothing hidden
adults.option_b: Some things are hidden from God
adults.option_c: God only sees outward actions
adults.option_d: God chooses not to see certain things
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:13 — "Neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight: but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do."

adults.question: What does "passed into the heavens" mean regarding Jesus?
adults.option_a: Jesus has passed through the heavens into God's very presence — paralleling the high priest entering the Holy of Holies, but into the true heavenly sanctuary
adults.option_b: Jesus merely ascended to the clouds
adults.option_c: Jesus went to one of the created heavens
adults.option_d: Jesus went to a physical location in the sky
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:14 — "Seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession."

adults.question: What is the significance of Christ being tempted "in all points" yet without sin?
adults.option_a: Christ's sinless perfection through genuine temptation qualifies him uniquely as our sympathetic high priest who fully understands human weakness
adults.option_b: His temptations were not real because he was God
adults.option_c: He was only tempted in a few specific areas
adults.option_d: His sinlessness means he cannot understand temptation
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:15 — "For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin."

adults.question: How does the author use Joshua to argue for a future rest?
adults.option_a: If Joshua had given them rest, God would not afterward have spoken of another day — therefore a rest yet remains
adults.option_b: Joshua fulfilled all the promises of rest
adults.option_c: Joshua's rest was identical to God's rest
adults.option_d: Joshua never entered the promised land
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:8 — "For if Jesus [Joshua] had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day."

adults.question: What exhortation does the author give about entering God's rest?
adults.option_a: Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief
adults.option_b: Let us rest from all effort immediately
adults.option_c: Let us wait passively for rest to come
adults.option_d: Let us earn our rest through perfect obedience
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:11 — "Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief."

adults.question: What does the phrase "the throne of grace" imply?
adults.option_a: God's throne is characterized by grace rather than judgment for those who approach through Christ — it is a place of mercy and timely help
adults.option_b: The throne is made of a graceful material
adults.option_c: Only certain people may approach it
adults.option_d: The throne dispenses both grace and wrath equally
adults.explanation: Hebrews 4:16 — "Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need."

kids.question: What kind of priest is Jesus called?
kids.option_a: A priest forever after the order of Melchisedec
kids.option_b: A priest of the temple in Jerusalem
kids.explanation: Hebrews 5:6 — "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec."

kids.question: Did Jesus learn to obey even though he is God's Son?
kids.option_a: Yes, he learned obedience by the things which he suffered
kids.option_b: No, he never had to learn anything
kids.explanation: Hebrews 5:8 — "Though he were a Son, yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered."

kids.question: What is the difference between milk and strong meat (solid food)?
kids.option_a: Milk is for beginners and strong meat is for those who are grown up in faith
kids.explanation: Hebrews 5:12-14 — "Ye have need that one teach you again... and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat."

teens.question: Where does a high priest come from?
teens.option_a: From among men — taken from among men and ordained for men in things pertaining to God
teens.option_c: From a special divine creation
teens.explanation: Hebrews 5:1 — "For every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins."

teens.question: Can a high priest choose this honour for himself?
teens.option_a: No man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God as was Aaron
teens.option_b: Yes, anyone who wants to can be high priest
teens.option_c: Only the firstborn son can choose it
teens.option_d: The people vote for who they want
teens.explanation: Hebrews 5:4 — "And no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron."

teens.question: What did Jesus offer up with strong crying and tears?
teens.option_a: Prayers and supplications unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared
teens.option_c: Gold and silver offerings
teens.explanation: Hebrews 5:7 — "Who in the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared."

teens.question: What did Jesus become after being made perfect?
teens.option_a: The author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him
teens.option_b: The ruler of all earthly kingdoms
teens.option_c: The commander of the angelic armies
teens.option_d: The judge who condemns all sinners
teens.explanation: Hebrews 5:9 — "And being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him."

teens.question: Why does the author scold the readers?
teens.option_a: By this time they ought to be teachers, but they need to be taught the first principles again — they need milk, not strong meat
teens.option_c: They had become too advanced
teens.option_d: They were giving too much money
teens.explanation: Hebrews 5:12 — "For when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God; and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat."

adults.question: What two qualities must a high priest have regarding sinners?
adults.option_a: He must be able to have compassion on the ignorant and on them that are out of the way, for he himself also is compassed with infirmity
adults.option_b: Perfect holiness and complete detachment
adults.option_c: Stern judgment and strict discipline
adults.option_d: Wealth and political influence
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:2 — "Who can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way; for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity."

adults.question: How does Christ's appointment as high priest parallel Aaron's?
adults.option_a: Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest — God said "Thou art my Son" and "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec"
adults.option_b: Christ appointed himself based on his divine nature
adults.option_c: Christ was appointed by the Sanhedrin
adults.option_d: Christ inherited the position from his earthly father
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:5-6 — "So also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest; but he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee. As he saith also in another place, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec."

adults.question: What does "learned he obedience by the things which he suffered" mean?
adults.option_a: Though eternally the Son, Christ experientially learned what obedience costs through his human suffering — not that he was previously disobedient
adults.option_b: He was disobedient before and suffering taught him to obey
adults.option_c: Suffering is the only way to learn anything
adults.option_d: His divine nature needed to be improved
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:8 — "Though he were a Son, yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered." This refers to experiential knowledge of obedience through suffering, not moral correction.

adults.question: What does it mean that Christ was "made perfect"?
adults.option_a: Being made perfect (teleiōtheis) — brought to completion or full qualification as the perfect Saviour through the completion of his earthly suffering and obedience
adults.option_b: He was morally imperfect and needed to improve
adults.option_c: He lacked divine attributes that suffering provided
adults.option_d: He was already perfect and nothing changed
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:9 — "And being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him." The word implies completion of his saving work, not moral improvement.

adults.question: What does the author mean by "strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age"?
adults.option_a: Mature believers who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil
adults.option_b: Only elderly Christians can understand deep doctrine
adults.option_c: Strong meat refers to literal food restrictions
adults.option_d: Full age means exactly thirty years old
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:14 — "But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil."

adults.question: What is significant about Christ being "called of God an high priest after the order of Melchisedec"?
adults.option_a: It establishes a priesthood that precedes and supersedes the Levitical order — not based on tribal descent but on divine appointment and eternal duration
adults.option_b: Melchisedec was Aaron's predecessor in the same priestly line
adults.option_c: This is merely a metaphorical comparison
adults.option_d: Melchisedec was actually an angel, not a human
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:10 — "Called of God an high priest after the order of Melchisedec." This introduces the argument developed in chapter 7.

adults.question: What rebuke does the author give about their spiritual condition?
adults.option_a: They are dull of hearing — by this time they should be teachers but instead need to be taught the first principles again
adults.option_b: They are too advanced for their own good
adults.option_c: They study too much and need to rest
adults.option_d: They are perfectly mature in their faith
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:11-12 — "Of whom we have many things to say, and hard to be uttered, seeing ye are dull of hearing. For when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God."

adults.question: What is the "first principles of the oracles of God" that the readers need again?
adults.option_a: The elementary teachings of God's word — they are unskilful in the word of righteousness, like babes needing milk
adults.option_b: Advanced theological debates
adults.option_c: Secret knowledge for the elite
adults.option_d: The Greek philosophical foundations
adults.explanation: Hebrews 5:12-13 — "Ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God; and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat. For every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness: for he is a babe."

kids.question: What does the author want the readers to do?
kids.option_a: Go on unto perfection (grow up in their faith)
kids.option_b: Stay as babies in their faith
kids.option_d: Go back to the very beginning again
kids.explanation: Hebrews 6:1 — "Therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection."

kids.question: Does God forget the good things we do for him?
kids.option_a: No, God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love
kids.option_b: Yes, God forgets everything we do
kids.option_c: He only remembers the bad things
kids.explanation: Hebrews 6:10 — "For God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love, which ye have shewed toward his name."

kids.question: What is our hope compared to?
kids.option_a: An anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast
kids.option_b: A kite flying in the wind
kids.option_c: A leaf blown by the breeze
kids.explanation: Hebrews 6:19 — "Which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast."

teens.question: What six foundation teachings does the author list?
teens.option_a: Repentance from dead works, faith toward God, doctrine of baptisms, laying on of hands, resurrection of the dead, eternal judgment
teens.option_b: Love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness
teens.option_c: Prayer, fasting, giving, worship, fellowship, study
teens.option_d: Creation, fall, flood, exodus, exile, return
teens.explanation: Hebrews 6:1-2 — "Not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works, and of faith toward God, Of the doctrine of baptisms, and of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead, and of eternal judgment."

teens.question: What does the author compare to land that receives rain and brings forth herbs?
teens.option_a: Those who receive God's blessings and produce good fruit — they receive blessing from God
teens.option_c: Those who travel to many places
teens.explanation: Hebrews 6:7 — "For the earth which drinketh in the rain that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs meet for them by whom it is dressed, receiveth blessing from God."

teens.question: What does the author want each believer to show?
teens.option_a: The same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end
teens.option_c: Great speaking ability
teens.explanation: Hebrews 6:11 — "And we desire that every one of you do shew the same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end."

teens.question: What two immutable (unchangeable) things give us strong consolation?
teens.option_a: God's promise and God's oath — it is impossible for God to lie
teens.option_b: Our own strength and our own wisdom
teens.option_c: The law and the prophets
teens.explanation: Hebrews 6:17-18 — "God, willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath: That by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie, we might have a strong consolation."

teens.question: Where does our anchor of hope enter?
teens.option_a: Within the veil — where Jesus as our forerunner has entered for us, a high priest after the order of Melchisedec
teens.option_b: Into the depths of the ocean
teens.option_c: Into the foundation of the earth
teens.option_d: Into the walls of the temple
teens.explanation: Hebrews 6:19-20 — "Which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast, and which entereth into that within the veil; Whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus, made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec."

adults.question: What does the author say is impossible regarding those who "fall away" in verses 4-6?
adults.option_a: It is impossible to renew them again unto repentance if they were once enlightened, tasted the heavenly gift, were made partakers of the Holy Ghost, tasted the good word and powers of the world to come, and then fell away
adults.option_b: It is impossible to sin after being saved
adults.option_c: It is impossible to understand the gospel without a teacher
adults.option_d: It is impossible to lose any spiritual gift once received
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:4-6 — "For it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost, And have tasted the good word of God, and the powers of the world to come, If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance."

adults.question: Why is it impossible to renew such people to repentance?
adults.option_a: They crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh and put him to an open shame
adults.option_b: Because God is not powerful enough
adults.option_c: Because the cross has limited effectiveness
adults.option_d: Because salvation can only be offered once
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:6 — "If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance; seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh, and put him to an open shame."

adults.question: What agricultural metaphor does the author use for apostasy?
adults.option_a: Land that beareth thorns and briers is rejected, nigh unto cursing, whose end is to be burned — contrasted with land that brings forth herbs and receives blessing
adults.option_b: A vine that produces sour grapes
adults.option_c: A tree that bears no leaves
adults.option_d: A field with no seeds planted
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:7-8 — "For the earth which drinketh in the rain... and bringeth forth herbs... receiveth blessing from God: But that which beareth thorns and briers is rejected, and is nigh unto cursing; whose end is to be burned."

adults.question: What is the author persuaded of regarding his readers despite the severe warning?
adults.option_a: Better things of them, and things that accompany salvation — though he thus speaks
adults.option_b: That they have already fallen away
adults.option_c: That they are beyond hope
adults.option_d: That most of them are unbelievers
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:9 — "But, beloved, we are persuaded better things of you, and things that accompany salvation, though we thus speak."

adults.question: What example does the author give of patient endurance?
adults.option_a: Abraham — who after he had patiently endured, obtained the promise
adults.option_b: Moses — who led Israel through the wilderness
adults.option_c: David — who waited to become king
adults.option_d: Job — who endured suffering
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:15 — "And so, after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise."

adults.question: Why did God confirm his promise with an oath?
adults.option_a: Willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel — since he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself
adults.option_b: Because his word alone was not trustworthy
adults.option_c: Because Abraham demanded an oath
adults.option_d: Because oaths were required by Jewish law
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:13,17 — "For when God made promise to Abraham, because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself... God, willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath."

adults.question: What does calling Jesus a "forerunner" who entered within the veil signify?
adults.option_a: Unlike the Levitical high priest who entered alone, Jesus entered as a forerunner — implying that others will follow him into God's presence
adults.option_b: Jesus entered but no one else can follow
adults.option_c: The veil still separates God from man
adults.option_d: Jesus entered a physical temple in heaven
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:20 — "Whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus, made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec." The term "forerunner" implies others will follow.

adults.question: What does the "anchor of the soul" represent theologically?
adults.option_a: Christian hope grounded in God's promise and oath, entering the heavenly sanctuary where Christ intercedes — providing absolute security and certainty despite life's storms
adults.option_b: A physical anchor used in worship
adults.option_c: A metaphor for earthly stability and wealth
adults.option_d: A symbol of remaining fixed in one location
adults.explanation: Hebrews 6:19 — "Which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast, and which entereth into that within the veil." The anchor connects the believer to the heavenly sanctuary.

kids.question: Who was Melchisedec?
kids.option_a: King of Salem and priest of the most high God
kids.explanation: Hebrews 7:1 — "For this Melchisedec, king of Salem, priest of the most high God."

kids.question: What great man gave a tenth of everything to Melchisedec?
kids.option_a: Abraham
kids.explanation: Hebrews 7:2 — "To whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all."

kids.question: Does Jesus ever stop being our priest?
kids.option_a: No, he continues forever as our priest
kids.option_b: Yes, he stopped when he went to heaven
kids.option_c: Yes, he passed it to someone else
kids.explanation: Hebrews 7:24 — "But this man, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood."

teens.question: What does the name Melchisedec mean?
teens.option_a: King of righteousness — and King of Salem means King of peace
teens.option_c: King of wisdom and learning
teens.explanation: Hebrews 7:2 — "First being by interpretation King of righteousness, and after that also King of Salem, which is, King of peace."

teens.question: How is Melchisedec described in terms of genealogy?
teens.option_a: Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days nor end of life, made like unto the Son of God
teens.option_b: Son of Aaron from the tribe of Levi
teens.option_c: Son of Abraham from the tribe of Judah
teens.option_d: A descendant of Adam through Seth
teens.explanation: Hebrews 7:3 — "Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life; but made like unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually."

teens.question: Why is the Melchisedec priesthood better than the Levitical?
teens.option_a: If perfection were by the Levitical priesthood, why was there need for another priest after the order of Melchisedec?
teens.option_b: The Levitical priests performed more sacrifices
teens.option_c: The Levitical priests were better educated
teens.option_d: The Levitical priesthood was newer and more relevant
teens.explanation: Hebrews 7:11 — "If therefore perfection were by the Levitical priesthood... what further need was there that another priest should rise after the order of Melchisedec, and not be called after the order of Aaron?"

teens.question: What does Jesus save us through because he lives forever?
teens.option_a: He is able to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them
teens.option_b: He saves only those who perform enough good works
teens.option_c: He saves only the Jewish people
teens.option_d: He saves only those who keep the law perfectly
teens.explanation: Hebrews 7:25 — "Wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them."

teens.question: What kind of high priest do we need?
teens.option_a: One who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens
teens.option_b: One who is wealthy and powerful
teens.option_c: One who is experienced in worldly affairs
teens.explanation: Hebrews 7:26 — "For such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens."

adults.question: What argument does the author make about Abraham paying tithes to Melchisedec?
adults.option_a: The less is blessed of the better — since Abraham (and Levi in his loins) paid tithes to Melchisedec, the Melchisedec priesthood is superior to the Levitical
adults.option_b: Abraham was forced to pay under duress
adults.option_c: The tithes were a temporary cultural custom
adults.option_d: Melchisedec was Abraham's servant
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:4,7,9-10 — "Now consider how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils... without all contradiction the less is blessed of the better... Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham."

adults.question: What change does the new priesthood necessitate?
adults.option_a: For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law
adults.option_b: The law remains exactly the same
adults.option_c: Only the sacrifices change, not the law
adults.option_d: The priesthood and law are unrelated
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:12 — "For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law."

adults.question: How is Christ's priesthood different from the Levitical?
adults.option_a: Not after the law of a carnal commandment, but after the power of an endless life
adults.option_b: Based on tribal descent from Levi
adults.option_c: Temporary and transferred to successors
adults.option_d: Requiring daily animal sacrifices
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:16 — "Who is made, not after the law of a carnal commandment, but after the power of an endless life."

adults.question: What does the author say the law made nothing perfect?
adults.option_a: The law made nothing perfect, but the bringing in of a better hope did, by the which we draw nigh unto God
adults.option_b: The law achieved complete perfection
adults.option_c: The law was the final revelation of God
adults.option_d: The law and the new covenant are identical
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:19 — "For the law made nothing perfect, but the bringing in of a better hope did; by the which we draw nigh unto God."

adults.question: What makes Jesus the surety of a better testament?
adults.option_a: He was made priest with an oath — the Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever — making him surety of a better testament
adults.option_b: He provided a large financial guarantee
adults.option_c: He signed a written agreement
adults.option_d: He was approved by the Sanhedrin
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:21-22 — "The Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec: By so much was Jesus made a surety of a better testament."

adults.question: Why is Christ's priesthood "unchangeable"?
adults.option_a: Because he continueth ever — the Greek "aparabatos" means untransferable, it cannot be passed to a successor because he lives forever
adults.option_b: Because no one wants to replace him
adults.option_c: Because the church voted to keep him
adults.option_d: Because the law forbids any change
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:24 — "But this man, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood." The word "aparabatos" means a priesthood that does not pass to another.

adults.question: What does "save them to the uttermost" mean?
adults.option_a: Completely, perfectly, and eternally — Christ's intercession is perpetual and his salvation is total for all who come to God through him
adults.option_b: Save only the most deserving believers
adults.option_c: Save temporarily until the next sacrifice
adults.option_d: Save partially, requiring additional works
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:25 — "Wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them."

adults.question: How does Christ's once-for-all sacrifice differ from the Levitical priests'?
adults.option_a: He needeth not daily to offer up sacrifice first for his own sins then for the people — he did this once when he offered up himself, being sinless
adults.option_b: He offers sacrifices more frequently than they did
adults.option_c: He offers the same type of animal sacrifices
adults.option_d: He must repeat his sacrifice periodically
adults.explanation: Hebrews 7:27 — "Who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, when he offered up himself."

kids.question: Where is Jesus serving as our high priest?
kids.option_a: In heaven, at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens
kids.option_c: In a tent in the wilderness
kids.option_d: In a church building on earth
kids.explanation: Hebrews 8:1 — "We have such an high priest, who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens."

kids.question: Did God make a new agreement (covenant) with his people?
kids.option_a: Yes, God made a new covenant that is better than the old one
kids.option_b: No, the old covenant is still the only one
kids.option_c: He made the same covenant again
kids.explanation: Hebrews 8:8 — "Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel."

kids.question: Where does God promise to write his laws under the new covenant?
kids.option_a: In our minds and in our hearts
kids.option_d: On the walls of the temple
kids.explanation: Hebrews 8:10 — "I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts."

teens.question: What is Jesus the minister of in heaven?
teens.option_a: The sanctuary and the true tabernacle which the Lord pitched, and not man
teens.option_c: A tent made by Moses
teens.explanation: Hebrews 8:2 — "A minister of the sanctuary, and of the true tabernacle, which the Lord pitched, and not man."

teens.question: What was the earthly tabernacle a copy of?
teens.option_a: The pattern showed to Moses in the mount — a shadow of heavenly things
teens.option_b: An original design by Moses himself
teens.option_c: A standard building of that era
teens.explanation: Hebrews 8:5 — "Who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount."

teens.question: What does the new covenant promise about knowing God?
teens.option_a: They shall not teach every man his neighbour saying Know the Lord, for all shall know me from the least to the greatest
teens.option_c: Knowledge of God will be limited to scholars
teens.option_d: People must be taught for many years to know God
teens.explanation: Hebrews 8:11 — "And they shall not teach every man his neighbour, and every man his brother, saying, Know the Lord: for all shall know me, from the least to the greatest."

teens.question: What does God promise about sins under the new covenant?
teens.option_a: I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more
teens.option_b: Sins will be remembered but forgiven partially
teens.option_c: Sins must be atoned for yearly by sacrifice
teens.option_d: Only small sins will be forgiven
teens.explanation: Hebrews 8:12 — "For I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more."

teens.question: What happened to the first covenant when God made a new one?
teens.option_a: He hath made the first old — that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away
teens.option_c: Both covenants continue with equal authority
teens.option_d: The first covenant was improved slightly
teens.explanation: Hebrews 8:13 — "In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away."

adults.question: What is the "sum" or main point of the author's argument?
adults.option_a: We have such an high priest who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens, a minister of the sanctuary and the true tabernacle
adults.option_b: The law must still be observed perfectly
adults.option_c: The earthly temple is the centre of worship
adults.option_d: Animal sacrifices remain necessary
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:1-2 — "Now of the things which we have spoken this is the sum: We have such an high priest, who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens; A minister of the sanctuary, and of the true tabernacle, which the Lord pitched, and not man."

adults.question: Why was a new covenant needed according to verse 7?
adults.option_a: If the first covenant had been faultless, then should no place have been sought for the second
adults.option_b: The first covenant was perfect and complete
adults.option_c: God changed his mind arbitrarily
adults.option_d: The people requested a new agreement
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:7 — "For if that first covenant had been faultless, then should no place have been sought for the second."

adults.question: Where does the author find fault with the first covenant?
adults.option_a: There was no fault with the first covenant
adults.option_b: The fault was in God's design
adults.option_c: The fault was in the wording of the covenant
adults.option_d: Finding fault with them — the people could not keep it; the new covenant addresses this by writing the law on hearts and minds
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:8 — "For finding fault with them, he saith, Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make a new covenant."

adults.question: What long prophecy does the author quote about the new covenant?
adults.option_a: Jeremiah 31:31-34 — the new covenant with Israel and Judah, laws in minds and hearts, universal knowledge of God, and complete forgiveness
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53 — the suffering servant
adults.option_c: Ezekiel 37 — the valley of dry bones
adults.option_d: Daniel 9 — the seventy weeks prophecy
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34, the longest Old Testament quotation in the New Testament.

adults.question: What does the new covenant being "not according to the covenant I made with their fathers" signify?
adults.option_a: A fundamentally different covenant — not merely a renewal of the old, because they broke the old covenant despite God's faithfulness
adults.option_b: A minor revision of the same agreement
adults.option_c: An extension of the same terms
adults.option_d: A temporary replacement until the old is restored
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:9 — "Not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt; because they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not, saith the Lord."

adults.question: What does Christ's "more excellent ministry" establish him as?
adults.option_a: The mediator of a better covenant, which was established upon better promises
adults.option_b: A mediator equal to Moses
adults.option_c: A mediator of the same covenant with different terms
adults.option_d: A temporary mediator until a better one comes
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:6 — "But now hath he obtained a more excellent ministry, by how much also he is the mediator of a better covenant, which was established upon better promises."

adults.question: What does the phrase "I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people" express?
adults.option_a: The covenant formula of intimate relationship — God claims his people as his own and they recognize him as their God, the heart of all biblical covenants
adults.option_b: A simple legal arrangement
adults.option_c: A temporary political alliance
adults.option_d: A conditional agreement based on performance
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:10 — "I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people." This is the covenant formula found throughout Scripture (cf. Exodus 6:7, Leviticus 26:12).

adults.question: What does the author conclude about the first covenant in verse 13?
adults.option_a: By saying "a new covenant" God has made the first old — what is obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away, implying the old covenant system was passing away
adults.option_b: The first covenant will be renewed and strengthened
adults.option_c: The first covenant is eternal and unchanging
adults.option_d: Both covenants will continue forever side by side
adults.explanation: Hebrews 8:13 — "In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away."

kids.question: What did Jesus use to enter the holy place instead of animal blood?
kids.option_a: His own blood
kids.explanation: Hebrews 9:12 — "Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us."

kids.question: How many times does a person die?
kids.option_a: Once — it is appointed unto men once to die
kids.option_d: As many times as they want
kids.explanation: Hebrews 9:27 — "And as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment."

kids.question: How many times did Jesus need to offer himself as a sacrifice?
kids.option_a: Once — he appeared once to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself
kids.option_b: Every year like the old priests
kids.explanation: Hebrews 9:26 — "But now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself."

teens.question: What was in the first tabernacle and the second (Holy of Holies)?
teens.option_a: The first had the candlestick, table, and shewbread; the second had the golden censer, the ark of the covenant covered with the mercy seat and cherubims
teens.option_c: The first had the ark and the second was empty
teens.explanation: Hebrews 9:2-5 describes the furnishings: "the first, wherein was the candlestick, and the table, and the shewbread... the Holiest of all; Which had the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant."

teens.question: How often did the high priest enter the Holy of Holies?
teens.option_a: Once every year alone, not without blood which he offered for himself and for the errors of the people
teens.option_c: Every week on the Sabbath
teens.explanation: Hebrews 9:7 — "But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood, which he offered for himself, and for the errors of the people."

teens.question: What did Christ obtain by entering with his own blood?
teens.option_a: Eternal redemption for us
teens.option_c: A promise of future salvation
teens.explanation: Hebrews 9:12 — "Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us."

teens.question: What does Christ's blood purge our conscience from?
teens.option_a: Dead works to serve the living God
teens.option_c: All emotional pain
teens.explanation: Hebrews 9:14 — "How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?"

teens.question: What comes after death for all people?
teens.option_a: The judgment — as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment
teens.option_c: Nothing at all happens
teens.option_d: A second chance to live again
teens.explanation: Hebrews 9:27 — "And as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment."

adults.question: What was the Holy Ghost signifying by the structure of the tabernacle?
adults.option_a: That the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest while the first tabernacle was yet standing
adults.option_b: That the tabernacle was the final form of worship
adults.option_c: That only priests could ever approach God
adults.option_d: That the structure had no deeper meaning
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:8 — "The Holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest, while as the first tabernacle was yet standing."

adults.question: How does the author contrast Christ's sacrifice with animal sacrifices?
adults.option_a: If the blood of bulls and goats sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh, how much more shall the blood of Christ purge your conscience from dead works
adults.option_b: Animal sacrifices were equally effective
adults.option_c: Christ's sacrifice was merely symbolic
adults.option_d: Both types of sacrifice achieve the same result
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:13-14 — "For if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh: How much more shall the blood of Christ... purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?"

adults.question: What does "without shedding of blood is no remission" establish?
adults.option_a: The absolute necessity of blood atonement — the shedding of blood is indispensable for the forgiveness of sins under God's economy
adults.option_b: Blood shedding is optional for forgiveness
adults.option_c: Only symbolic blood is needed
adults.option_d: Forgiveness requires good works, not blood
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:22 — "And almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission."

adults.question: Where did Christ enter to appear in the presence of God for us?
adults.option_a: Into heaven itself — not into the holy places made with hands which are the figures of the true
adults.option_b: Into the earthly temple in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Into a replica of the tabernacle
adults.option_d: Into a heavenly tabernacle made with hands
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:24 — "For Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands, which are the figures of the true; but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us."

adults.question: Why did Christ not need to offer himself often?
adults.option_a: Then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world — but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself
adults.option_b: His sacrifice was only partially effective
adults.option_c: Multiple offerings would increase his merit
adults.option_d: The first offering was merely a trial
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:25-26 — "Nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others; For then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world: but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself."

adults.question: What does the author say about a testament (covenant) and death?
adults.option_a: Where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator — a testament is of force after men are dead
adults.option_b: Testaments can be enacted without death
adults.option_c: Death is irrelevant to covenant making
adults.option_d: Any person can activate a testament at any time
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:16-17 — "For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator. For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth."

adults.question: What three appearances of Christ does the author describe?
adults.option_a: He appeared once to put away sin (past), now appears in the presence of God for us (present), and shall appear the second time without sin unto salvation (future)
adults.option_b: He appeared only once in history
adults.option_c: He appears continuously in the temple
adults.option_d: He will not appear again
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:26,24,28 describes Christ's past appearance for sacrifice, present appearance before God, and future appearance for salvation.

adults.question: What does "Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many" connect to?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 53:12 — the Suffering Servant who bore the sins of many; his second coming will be apart from sin, unto salvation for those who look for him
adults.option_b: A general teaching about moral improvement
adults.option_c: A reference to the Day of Atonement goat only
adults.option_d: A political statement about liberation
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:28 — "So Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many; and unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation." This echoes Isaiah 53:12.

kids.question: Can the blood of bulls and goats really take away sins?
kids.option_a: No, it is not possible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins
kids.option_d: Only if you use enough blood
kids.explanation: Hebrews 10:4 — "For it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins."

kids.question: Should we stop meeting together as Christians?
kids.option_a: No — we should not forsake the assembling of ourselves together
kids.explanation: Hebrews 10:25 — "Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together."

kids.question: What should we hold fast to without wavering?
kids.option_a: The profession of our faith — because God is faithful who promised
kids.explanation: Hebrews 10:23 — "Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; (for he is faithful that promised.)"

teens.question: What did the old sacrifices remind people of every year?
teens.option_a: Their sins — there is a remembrance again made of sins every year
teens.option_c: Their wealth and prosperity
teens.explanation: Hebrews 10:3 — "But in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year."

teens.question: What did Jesus say when he came into the world?
teens.option_a: Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared me — Lo, I come to do thy will, O God
teens.option_b: I come to conquer the nations
teens.option_c: I come to build a great temple
teens.option_d: I come to establish an earthly kingdom
teens.explanation: Hebrews 10:5,7 — "Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared me... Lo, I come (in the volume of the book it is written of me,) to do thy will, O God."

teens.question: What new way has Jesus opened for us?
teens.option_a: A new and living way through the veil, that is to say, his flesh
teens.option_c: A new trade route to Rome
teens.option_d: A new path through the wilderness
teens.explanation: Hebrews 10:20 — "By a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh."

teens.question: What should we consider about one another?
teens.option_a: How to provoke one another unto love and to good works
teens.option_b: How to compete with each other
teens.option_c: How to judge one another's faults
teens.explanation: Hebrews 10:24 — "And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works."

teens.question: What is described as a fearful thing?
teens.option_a: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God
teens.option_b: It is fearful to speak in public
teens.option_c: It is fearful to travel the seas
teens.option_d: It is fearful to face wild animals
teens.explanation: Hebrews 10:31 — "It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God."

adults.question: What does the law having "a shadow of good things to come" mean?
adults.option_a: The law was a foreshadowing or type of the heavenly realities fulfilled in Christ — not the very image, so it could never make comers thereunto perfect
adults.option_b: The law was the final and complete revelation
adults.option_c: The shadow was superior to the reality
adults.option_d: The law had no connection to Christ
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:1 — "For the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect."

adults.question: What Psalm does the author quote about God's desire versus sacrifice?
adults.option_a: Psalm 51 — The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit
adults.option_b: Psalm 40:6-8 — "Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared me... Lo, I come to do thy will, O God"
adults.option_c: Psalm 23 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.option_d: Psalm 110 — Sit thou at my right hand
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes Psalm 40:6-8, showing Christ's willing obedience replacing animal sacrifices.

adults.question: What is the result of the one offering of the body of Jesus Christ?
adults.option_a: We are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all
adults.option_b: We are partially cleansed needing further sacrifices
adults.option_c: We are given a chance to earn our own salvation
adults.option_d: We receive temporary forgiveness only
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:10 — "By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all."

adults.question: What did Christ do after offering one sacrifice for sins forever?
adults.option_a: He sat down on the right hand of God, from henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool
adults.option_b: He continued to offer daily sacrifices
adults.option_c: He returned to earth immediately
adults.option_d: He entered a state of rest and inactivity
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:12-13 — "But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God; From henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool."

adults.question: What does the author say about wilful sin after receiving the knowledge of the truth?
adults.option_a: There remaineth no more sacrifice for sins, but a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation which shall devour the adversaries
adults.option_b: Another sacrifice will be provided
adults.option_c: Willful sin carries no consequences
adults.option_d: God will simply overlook it
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:26-27 — "For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins, But a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries."

adults.question: What sorer punishment does the author warn about?
adults.option_a: He that despised Moses' law died without mercy under two or three witnesses — of how much sorer punishment shall he be thought worthy who hath trodden under foot the Son of God
adults.option_b: The punishment is identical to Moses' law
adults.option_c: There is no punishment under the new covenant
adults.option_d: Only a mild rebuke is warranted
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:28-29 — "He that despised Moses' law died without mercy under two or three witnesses: Of how much sorer punishment, suppose ye, shall he be thought worthy, who hath trodden under foot the Son of God, and hath counted the blood of the covenant, wherewith he was sanctified, an unholy thing."

adults.question: What does the author remind them about their earlier faith?
adults.option_a: They endured a great fight of afflictions, were made a gazingstock by reproaches and afflictions, had compassion on prisoners, and took joyfully the spoiling of their goods knowing they had in heaven a better and enduring substance
adults.option_b: They had always lived comfortable lives
adults.option_c: They had never faced any persecution
adults.option_d: They had compromised their faith from the beginning
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:32-34 — "But call to remembrance the former days, in which, after ye were illuminated, ye endured a great fight of afflictions... and took joyfully the spoiling of your goods, knowing in yourselves that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance."

adults.question: What Old Testament quote does the author use about the just living by faith?
adults.option_a: The just shall live by faith — from Habakkuk 2:3-4; and if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 6:4 — Hear O Israel
adults.option_c: Genesis 15:6 — Abraham believed God
adults.option_d: Psalm 23:1 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.explanation: Hebrews 10:38 — "Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him." This quotes Habakkuk 2:3-4.

kids.question: What is faith?
kids.option_a: The substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen
kids.option_b: Believing only what you can see
kids.explanation: Hebrews 11:1 — "Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen."

kids.question: What did Noah build by faith?
kids.option_a: An ark to save his family
kids.explanation: Hebrews 11:7 — "By faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark to the saving of his house."

kids.question: What did Abraham do when God told him to go to a new place?
kids.option_a: He obeyed and went out, not knowing where he was going
kids.option_b: He refused to leave his home
kids.option_c: He asked for a map first
kids.explanation: Hebrews 11:8 — "By faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, obeyed; and he went out, not knowing whither he went."

teens.question: What do we understand by faith about the worlds?
teens.option_a: The worlds were framed by the word of God, so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear
teens.option_b: The world created itself by accident
teens.option_c: The world has always existed
teens.option_d: We cannot know anything about creation
teens.explanation: Hebrews 11:3 — "Through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear."

teens.question: What happened to Enoch by faith?
teens.option_a: He was translated (taken up) that he should not see death, because God had testified he pleased God
teens.option_b: He became the oldest person ever
teens.option_c: He built a city in God's name
teens.explanation: Hebrews 11:5 — "By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him: for before his translation he had this testimony, that he pleased God."

teens.question: What is impossible without faith?
teens.option_a: It is impossible to please God — for he that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
teens.option_b: It is impossible to earn a living
teens.option_c: It is impossible to make friends
teens.option_d: It is impossible to travel safely
teens.explanation: Hebrews 11:6 — "But without faith it is impossible to please him: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him."

teens.question: What did Moses choose by faith?
teens.option_a: Rather to suffer affliction with the people of God than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season
teens.option_b: To stay in Pharaoh's palace forever
teens.option_c: To become pharaoh of Egypt
teens.option_d: To ignore God's call and live comfortably
