teens.explanation: Hebrews 11:25 — "Choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God, than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season."

teens.question: Did these faithful heroes receive what was promised?
teens.option_a: No — they all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off
teens.option_b: Yes, they all received everything immediately
teens.option_c: Only some of them received the promises
teens.option_d: They didn't care about the promises
teens.explanation: Hebrews 11:13 — "These all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off, and were persuaded of them, and embraced them, and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth."

adults.question: What two key elements define faith in verse 1?
adults.option_a: The substance (hupostasis — assurance/confidence) of things hoped for, and the evidence (elegchos — conviction/proof) of things not seen
adults.option_b: Blind acceptance and emotional feeling
adults.option_c: Intellectual agreement and verbal confession
adults.option_d: Ritual observance and legal compliance
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:1 — "Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen." The Greek words imply confident assurance and inner conviction.

adults.question: What does the author say the elders obtained by faith?
adults.option_a: A good report — by faith the elders obtained a good report
adults.option_b: Great wealth and power
adults.option_c: Freedom from all suffering
adults.option_d: Immediate fulfilment of all promises
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:2 — "For by it the elders obtained a good report."

adults.question: What did Abraham look for by faith?
adults.option_a: A city which hath foundations, whose builder and maker is God
adults.option_b: A large estate in Canaan
adults.option_c: A palace in Mesopotamia
adults.option_d: A kingdom on earth
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:10 — "For he looked for a city which hath foundations, whose builder and maker is God."

adults.question: What did Moses esteem greater than the treasures in Egypt?
adults.option_a: The reproach of Christ — for he had respect unto the recompence of the reward
adults.option_b: The gold of Solomon's temple
adults.option_c: The approval of Pharaoh
adults.option_d: The wisdom of Egyptian scholars
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:26 — "Esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt: for he had respect unto the recompence of the reward."

adults.question: What did the faith heroes endure according to verses 35-38?
adults.option_a: Torture not accepting deliverance, mockings, scourgings, bonds, imprisonment, stoning, being sawn asunder, slain with the sword, destitute, afflicted, tormented — of whom the world was not worthy
adults.option_b: Comfortable and prosperous lives
adults.option_c: Only minor inconveniences
adults.option_d: Temporary setbacks followed by great success
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:35-38 — "Others were tortured... had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings... bonds and imprisonment: They were stoned, they were sawn asunder... being destitute, afflicted, tormented; (Of whom the world was not worthy.)"

adults.question: Why had these heroes not received the promise?
adults.option_a: God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect
adults.option_b: God forgot his promise to them
adults.option_c: Their faith was insufficient
adults.option_d: The promise was withdrawn
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:40 — "God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect."

adults.question: What does "by faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king" reveal about Moses?
adults.option_a: Moses endured as seeing him who is invisible — his faith enabled him to look beyond the visible threat of Pharaoh to the invisible reality of God
adults.option_b: Moses was unaware of any danger
adults.option_c: Moses had a powerful army to protect him
adults.option_d: Moses was not afraid of anything natural or supernatural
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:27 — "By faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king: for he endured, as seeing him who is invisible."

adults.question: What does the phrase "of whom the world was not worthy" mean?
adults.option_a: The world did not deserve these faithful believers — their suffering was not a sign of unworthiness but of the world's unworthiness of them
adults.option_b: The world rejected them because they were unimportant
adults.option_c: They were too good for any earthly community
adults.option_d: The world had no use for religious people
adults.explanation: Hebrews 11:38 — "(Of whom the world was not worthy:) they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth."

kids.question: Who should we look to as we run our race of faith?
kids.option_a: Jesus — the author and finisher of our faith
kids.explanation: Hebrews 12:2 — "Looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith."

kids.question: Why does God correct (discipline) us?
kids.option_a: Because he loves us, like a good father
kids.option_b: Because he is angry at us
kids.option_c: Because he wants to hurt us
kids.option_d: Because he doesn't care about us
kids.explanation: Hebrews 12:6 — "For whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth."

kids.question: What should we try to have with everyone?
kids.option_a: Peace — follow peace with all men
kids.explanation: Hebrews 12:14 — "Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord."

teens.question: What are we surrounded by according to this chapter?
teens.option_a: A great cloud of witnesses — the faithful heroes from chapter 11
teens.option_c: A great crowd of strangers
teens.explanation: Hebrews 12:1 — "Wherefore seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses."

teens.question: What should we lay aside to run the race?
teens.option_a: Every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us, running with patience
teens.option_c: Our friendships and relationships
teens.explanation: Hebrews 12:1 — "Let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us."

teens.question: What did Jesus endure for the joy set before him?
teens.option_a: The cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God
teens.option_c: A comfortable and pleasant life
teens.explanation: Hebrews 12:2 — "Who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God."

teens.question: What does God's chastening (discipline) produce afterward?
teens.option_a: The peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby
teens.option_c: Confusion and doubt
teens.explanation: Hebrews 12:11 — "Now no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous: nevertheless afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby."

teens.question: What does the author say our God is?
teens.option_a: A consuming fire
teens.option_c: An indifferent force
teens.explanation: Hebrews 12:29 — "For our God is a consuming fire."

adults.question: What does "looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith" mean?
adults.option_a: Jesus is both the originator (archēgos) and perfecter (teleiōtēs) of faith — he began it and will bring it to completion
adults.option_b: We should literally stare at a picture of Jesus
adults.option_c: Jesus wrote the Bible by himself
adults.option_d: Jesus will finish building the church building
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:2 — "Looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith; who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God."

adults.question: What does the author argue about chastening proving sonship?
adults.option_a: If ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards and not sons — discipline proves legitimate sonship
adults.option_b: Chastisement is a sign of God's rejection
adults.option_c: Only sinners receive God's discipline
adults.option_d: Discipline is never from God
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:8 — "But if ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards, and not sons."

adults.question: What contrast does the author draw between Sinai and Sion?
adults.option_a: They are not come to the mount that burned with fire and darkness and tempest (Sinai), but unto mount Sion, the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to an innumerable company of angels
adults.option_b: Sinai and Sion are the same place
adults.option_c: Sion is a physical mountain in Israel
adults.option_d: Sinai is the better destination
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:18,22 — "For ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched, and that burned with fire... But ye are come unto mount Sion, and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to an innumerable company of angels."

adults.question: Who does the author say believers have come to at mount Sion?
adults.option_a: God the Judge of all, the spirits of just men made perfect, Jesus the mediator of the new covenant, and the blood of sprinkling that speaketh better things than that of Abel
adults.option_b: Moses and the prophets only
adults.option_c: Angels and cherubim only
adults.option_d: The patriarchs and their families only
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:23-24 — "To God the Judge of all, and to the spirits of just men made perfect, And to Jesus the mediator of the new covenant, and to the blood of sprinkling, that speaketh better things than that of Abel."

adults.question: What does Abel's blood cry versus Christ's blood?
adults.option_a: Abel's blood cried for vengeance; Christ's blood speaks better things — forgiveness and reconciliation
adults.option_b: Both cry for the same thing
adults.option_c: Abel's blood is silent
adults.option_d: Christ's blood cries for judgment
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:24 — "The blood of sprinkling, that speaketh better things than that of Abel." Abel's blood cried from the ground for justice (Genesis 4:10); Christ's blood speaks of mercy and forgiveness.

adults.question: What does "yet once more" signify about the shaking?
adults.option_a: The removing of those things that are shaken — things that are made — that those things which cannot be shaken may remain, referring to the eternal kingdom
adults.option_b: A minor earthquake with no significance
adults.option_c: A political upheaval in Rome
adults.option_d: A temporary disruption soon resolved
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:27 — "And this word, Yet once more, signifieth the removing of those things that are shaken, as of things that are made, that those things which cannot be shaken may remain."

adults.question: What does the author urge about receiving a kingdom that cannot be moved?
adults.option_a: Let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear
adults.option_b: Let us celebrate with worldly festivities
adults.option_c: Let us rest from all service
adults.option_d: Let us demand our rights as citizens
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:28 — "Wherefore we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved, let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear."

adults.question: What does "follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord" teach?
adults.option_a: Both peace and holiness are essential — peace with others and personal holiness are not optional for those who will see God
adults.option_b: Only peace matters, not holiness
adults.option_c: Only holiness matters, not peace
adults.option_d: Neither peace nor holiness is required to see God
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:14 — "Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord."

kids.question: What should we continue to show?
kids.option_a: Brotherly love — let brotherly love continue
kids.explanation: Hebrews 13:1 — "Let brotherly love continue."

kids.question: Does Jesus ever change?
kids.option_a: No — Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever
kids.option_b: Yes, he changes all the time
kids.option_d: Only God the Father never changes
kids.explanation: Hebrews 13:8 — "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever."

kids.question: What has God promised he will never do?
kids.option_a: He will never leave us nor forsake us
kids.option_b: He will never listen to our prayers
kids.option_c: He will never help us again
kids.explanation: Hebrews 13:5 — "For he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee."

teens.question: What might happen when we entertain strangers?
teens.option_a: Some have entertained angels unawares
teens.option_b: We might lose all our money
teens.option_c: We might get in trouble with the law
teens.explanation: Hebrews 13:2 — "Be not forgetful to entertain strangers: for thereby some have entertained angels unawares."

teens.question: What does the author say about marriage?
teens.option_a: Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled; but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge
teens.option_b: Marriage is only for the wealthy
teens.option_c: Marriage is outdated and unnecessary
teens.option_d: Marriage is honourable only for priests
teens.explanation: Hebrews 13:4 — "Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge."

teens.question: What should our conversation (way of life) be free from?
teens.option_a: Covetousness — be content with such things as ye have, for God hath said I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
teens.option_c: All forms of happiness
teens.explanation: Hebrews 13:5 — "Let your conversation be without covetousness; and be content with such things as ye have: for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee."

teens.question: What sacrifice does God want us to offer continually?
teens.option_a: The sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name
teens.option_c: Burnt offerings of grain and oil
teens.explanation: Hebrews 13:15 — "By him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name."

teens.question: What can we boldly say because God will never leave us?
teens.option_a: The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me
teens.option_b: I am the greatest person alive
teens.option_c: I will never have any problems
teens.option_d: I will always be wealthy and comfortable
teens.explanation: Hebrews 13:6 — "So that we may boldly say, The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me."

adults.question: What does "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever" affirm?
adults.option_a: The immutability and eternality of Christ — he does not change in his nature, character, or purposes across all time
adults.option_b: Jesus adapts his character to each generation
adults.option_c: This refers only to his earthly ministry
adults.option_d: Christ changes with the times
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:8 — "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever." This verse affirms Christ's divine immutability.

adults.question: What does "let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach" mean?
adults.option_a: Believers should be willing to separate from established religious systems and bear the social cost of identifying with Christ, as he suffered outside the gate
adults.option_b: We should literally live outside the city walls
adults.option_c: We should abandon all community
adults.option_d: We should join a different religion
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:13 — "Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach." Jesus suffered outside the gate of Jerusalem (v.12); believers follow him outside human religious systems.

adults.question: What does "we have an altar" refer to?
adults.option_a: Christ's cross as the altar from which the new covenant community feeds — those who serve the old tabernacle have no right to eat from this altar
adults.option_b: A physical altar in a church building
adults.option_c: The altar of incense in the temple
adults.option_d: A metaphorical table of fellowship
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:10 — "We have an altar, whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle."

adults.question: Why did Jesus suffer without the gate?
adults.option_a: That he might sanctify the people with his own blood
adults.option_b: Because there was no room inside the city
adults.option_c: Because Roman law required it
adults.option_d: Because the temple was too crowded
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:12 — "Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate."

adults.question: What two types of sacrifices does the author urge?
adults.option_a: The sacrifice of praise (fruit of lips giving thanks) and doing good and communicating (sharing), for with such sacrifices God is well pleased
adults.option_b: Animal sacrifices and grain offerings
adults.option_c: Fasting and prayer only
adults.option_d: Financial tithes and building projects
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:15-16 — "By him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name. But to do good and to communicate forget not: for with such sacrifices God is well pleased."

adults.question: What does the author say about obeying spiritual leaders?
adults.option_a: Obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves, for they watch for your souls as they that must give account — that they may do it with joy and not with grief
adults.option_b: Spiritual leaders have no authority at all
adults.option_c: Leaders should never be questioned or obeyed
adults.option_d: Leaders are accountable to no one
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:17 — "Obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves: for they watch for your souls, as they that must give account, that they may do it with joy, and not with grief: for that is unprofitable for you."

adults.question: What benediction does the author give about the God of peace?
adults.option_a: The God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant, make you perfect in every good work to do his will
adults.option_b: May the God of war give you victory
adults.option_c: May the God of wisdom grant you knowledge
adults.option_d: May the God of abundance make you wealthy
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:20-21 — "Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant, Make you perfect in every good work to do his will."

adults.question: What does the phrase "the blood of the everlasting covenant" signify?
adults.option_a: Christ's blood ratifies an eternal covenant — unlike the temporary Mosaic covenant, this covenant is everlasting because it is sealed by the blood of the eternal Son
adults.option_b: The blood of animals that was shed repeatedly
adults.option_c: A symbolic reference with no real substance
adults.option_d: The blood of the martyrs throughout history
adults.explanation: Hebrews 13:20 — "Through the blood of the everlasting covenant." This phrase connects Christ's death to the establishment of an eternal covenant that supersedes all previous covenants.
